planting.note: Plant fresh seeds immediately after collecting in fall, 1-2 inches deep in moist, humus-rich soil; painted buckeye seeds lose viability quickly and should not be allowed to dry out before planting. Plant in a location with at least some shade.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; painted buckeye is a native small tree or large shrub of the southeastern US Piedmont and Coastal Plain, naturally growing in the understory of moist woodland slopes and stream banks. It is the most heat-tolerant native buckeye, making it an excellent choice for southern gardens where the more common red buckeye (A. pavia) and Ohio buckeye (A. glabra) can struggle in summer heat. The pale cream to yellow flower clusters in spring attract ruby-throated hummingbirds and large bees. Like all buckeyes, the large, shiny seeds are toxic to humans and livestock, but are an important food source for squirrels which chew through the outer covering to reach the seed. The foliage often develops excellent yellow fall color.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; New Zealand tea tree requires excellent drainage and will not survive in heavy, poorly drained soil. Propagate from semi-hardwood cuttings in summer.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, lean to average soil in full sun; New Zealand tea tree is one of the most important plants of Maori tradition and modern apiculture — the intensely antibacterial nectar from manuka flowers produces the famous manuka honey, scientifically documented for its exceptional wound-healing and antibacterial properties. The leaves were used by Maori as a medicinal tea. In the garden, manuka is a handsome small to medium shrub covered in masses of small, open flowers — white in the species, pink to deep crimson in many cultivars — in late winter through spring. The tiny, heather-like aromatic leaves are evergreen. Many ornamental double-flowered cultivars are available ('Martini', 'Red Damask', 'Crimson Glory') that are showy but produce less nectar for bees. Full drainage is essential — root rot in wet soil is the primary cause of failure.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; tropical snowball establishes quickly in warm weather and may bloom within 2 years from cutting-grown plants. It dies back after hard frost but re-sprouts from the roots in zone 9.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade in zones 9-12; tropical snowball is one of the most spectacular flowering small trees for warm-climate gardens, producing enormous, round clusters of soft pink to deep pink flowers in late winter and spring when little else is in bloom. The large, heart-shaped leaves are bold and tropical. The pendant flower clusters hang like ornaments from the branches and are extraordinarily fragrant — filling the surrounding air with a sweet, honeyed perfume. Native to Madagascar, the tree is now widely planted in tropical and subtropical gardens worldwide. In zone 9, it may be damaged by hard freezes but typically re-sprouts vigorously from the base in spring, behaving as a large perennial shrub. It is a magnet for butterflies and bees. Prune after flowering to maintain a tidy shape.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; Chinese witchhazel is slow to establish and resents root disturbance — container plants are far more reliable than bare-root. Grafted plants are also available and bloom sooner.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Chinese witchhazel is one of the most fragrant of the witchhazels, producing its spidery yellow to orange flowers in the dead of winter (January through February) with an intensely sweet, warm, spicy scent that carries remarkable distances on cold air. The flowers are frost-resistant and remain attractive even after mild snow and ice events. Chinese witchhazel (H. mollis) is a primary parent of the popular Hamamelis x intermedia hybrids that dominate nursery trade — including 'Arnold Promise' (yellow), 'Jelena' (orange), and 'Diane' (coppery-red). The large, rounded leaves turn a clear golden-yellow in fall. Slow to establish but very long-lived and low-maintenance once settled.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade; fragrant snowbell is slow to establish and benefits from mulching to maintain consistent soil moisture during the first 2-3 years.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant snowbell is an underused Japanese deciduous tree distinguished from the common Japanese snowbell (S. japonicus) by its much larger, broadly rounded leaves that can be 8 inches across — giving the tree a bold, almost tropical appearance in summer. In late spring, the long, pendant racemes of white, bell-shaped flowers hang beneath the large leaves in an unusually graceful display with a soft vanilla-like fragrance that is notably stronger than S. japonicus. The combination of bold leaves and hanging flower clusters is architecturally distinctive. The tree tolerates partial shade better than most flowering trees and is excellent as an understory specimen beneath taller oaks and beeches. Yellow fall color.
